Subject: Keep the Savings
Body:
There’s a moment in every maturing design system where the
efficiency gains become visible. Leadership notices delivery
speeding up. Planning gets easier. The roadmap suddenly feels
lighter. What happens next often determines whether those gains
help the team, or simply reset expectations.

I’ve seen this play out more than once – a team builds a system
that cuts delivery time in half, and within a couple of planning
cycles, sprint commitments double. The time saved doesn’t go to
maintenance, documentation, or improving quality. It disappears
into throughput.

Speed isn’t the problem. Treating speed as the point is.

Design system teams often sell efficiency to get buy-in in the
first place. That’s understandable. But if speed is how the
system is justified, speed becomes the expectation – and every
gain quickly turns into the new baseline.

In those environments, teams adapt. They ship on the original
timelines and use the slack for the work that never makes the
roadmap: stabilizing components, mentoring partners, paying down
system debt, or simply working at a pace that doesn’t burn people
out. It’s not strategy, it’s survival.

The longer-term work is reframing what the system is for. Not
just faster delivery, but resilience, sustainability, and product
quality over time. That shift isn’t always possible everywhere.

When it isn’t, protecting the system – and the people maintaining
it – matters. And when that stops being enough, it’s usually a
signal worth listening to.

Subject: When Teams Stop Calling
Body:
Design system teams rarely set out to become enforcers. But as
review queues grow and more work funnels through approvals, the
team’s role can narrow to catching problems. When that happens,
the relationships tend to change alongside it.

When a system team becomes known mainly for reviewing work and
flagging issues, product teams start to delay involving them. Not
out of malice, but out of self-preservation. By the time the
system team is brought in, small issues have already grown, and
the conversation naturally centers on what’s wrong. Over time,
that becomes the team’s reputation – and people stop calling
altogether.

The distinction between standards work and systems work helps
explain why. Standards teams assess finished work. Systems teams
influence how work gets built. The difference isn’t just
responsibility – it’s how teams experience support while
decisions are still being made.

When teams start holding work back, that’s diagnostic. Somewhere
along the way, the relationship shifted from a partnership to
policing. And better components or clearer documentation won’t
fix that on their own.

The way forward usually starts with changing how the system team
shows up. Being involved earlier. Offering help before it’s
requested. Paying attention to what slows teams down and removing
friction before it turns into feedback.

Systems work is relationship work. The components matter, but so
does whether teams feel comfortable reaching out before things
are finished.

Subject: Design System Relief
Body:
Efficiency and consistency are the metrics design system teams
usually talk about. Faster delivery. Fewer variations. Less
rework.

Those matter. But they miss something just as important.

Relief.

Well-functioning design systems reduce the cognitive and
emotional load on teams. Fewer decisions to second-guess. Fewer
arguments to relitigate. Less anxiety about whether something is
“right”. Work feels lighter, even when the work itself hasn’t
changed much.

When that relief isn’t there, it shows.

Teams hesitate. They work around the system instead of with it.
They rebuild things they don’t trust. The system may be
technically sound, but it doesn’t feel supportive. Adoption
stalls – not because the system is bad, but because it doesn’t
remove enough weight.

This is why relief and adoption tend to move together.

When teams feel relief, they come back. They reuse components
without debate. They trust that the system has their back. Those
behaviors are measurable, even if the feeling itself isn’t.

Relief shows up in what teams stop doing. Fewer Slack threads
about edge cases. Less time spent justifying decisions. Fewer
“are we allowed to do this?” questions. More default use, less
deliberation.

Design systems that focus only on efficiency often miss this.
They optimize for speed without addressing the friction that made
the work stressful in the first place. The system might help
teams ship faster, but still leave them carrying the same mental
load.

A system that’s doing its job doesn’t just make work quicker. It
makes it easier to breathe.

If people don’t feel some sense of relief, the work isn’t
finished yet.

Subject: Subtract to Scale
Body:
Most design system roadmaps keep expanding. More components, more
patterns, more documentation. The backlog grows, the system
sprawls, and over time it starts to feel less like a toolkit and
more like an archive of everything anyone ever asked for.

At that point, adoption becomes harder, not easier – not because
teams need more, but because the system is carrying too much.

The path forward might be subtraction.

Consider the math: If you have 100 components and 70 are actively
used, that’s a 70% adoption rate. Remove 10 unused components and
you’re at 77%, without anyone adopting anything new. Nothing
changed except what you chose to keep.

This isn’t an accounting trick. Those components still carry real
cost: maintenance, documentation, accessibility checks,
compatibility testing, and the ongoing effort of supporting
patterns no one is using. Cutting them frees capacity for work
that actually matters.

The harder part is the decision itself. Teams add components
because someone asked for them, and saying yes feels helpful.
Removing something feels like failure – or like admitting a past
decision didn’t age well. But unused components don’t stay
neutral. Over time, they become clutter dressed up as
comprehensiveness.

A mature system isn’t defined by how much it contains. It’s
defined by how deliberately it’s maintained – including what it’s
willing to let go.

Subject: Chronicling System Decisions
Body:
Design system documentation usually focuses on describing what
exists – what a component does, how it behaves, where it should
be used.

That information is necessary. It’s also incomplete.

Teams don’t just need to know what a component is. They need to
understand why it exists, what problem it was meant to solve, and
what trade-offs were made along the way. Without that context,
the system becomes a set of rules without a story.

This is where chronicling comes in.

Chronicling captures the reasoning behind decisions, not just the
decisions themselves. It records why one approach was chosen over
another, what constraints were in play, and how the component
evolved. That context helps teams make better judgments when they
encounter situations the documentation doesn’t explicitly cover.

Documentation creates reference material. Chronicling creates
understanding. A healthy system needs both.

When that context is missing, teams end up recreating the same
conversations. Questions surface in Slack that aren’t really
about usage, but about intent. Chronicling reduces that
back-and-forth because the reasoning is already there.

The timing matters.

The most useful chronicles are created alongside the work, while
decisions are still being made. When chronicling is treated as an
afterthought, it rarely happens at all. Over time, this shared
record becomes less about individual components and more about
how the system thinks.

Design systems don’t just ship artifacts. They carry decisions
across an organization. Chronicling is how those decisions remain
understandable as teams and context change.

Subject: On Implicit Systems
Body:
Many design systems work because people know how they’re supposed
to be used.

They know which component to reach for. They know what a token
really means. They know which rules are flexible and which ones
aren’t.

That kind of shared understanding can carry a system surprisingly
far. It’s efficient. It feels lightweight. And early on, it often
works better than formal structure.

Until it doesn’t.

As systems grow, that “just knowing” becomes harder to maintain.
Teams change. Context gets lost. New people arrive without the
same history. Decisions that once lived in conversation start to
drift. What used to feel obvious becomes inconsistent, then
confusing.

At that point, the system hasn’t broken – it’s been exposed.

Exposure tends to happen when context drops away. When decisions
have to stand on their own, without the background knowledge that
once made them feel clear.

That’s why AI tools have become such a useful stress test for
design systems.

Unlike people, machines don’t infer intent. They don’t carry
history. They don’t “just know” what a component is for or which
token should be used. They rely entirely on what the system makes
explicit. When meaning is vague or implicit, the cracks show
immediately.

What AI surfaces isn’t a new problem. It’s the same one teams
have been working around for years, just without the buffer of
human interpretation.

Design systems scale when shared understanding moves out of
people’s heads and into the system itself. Not all at once, and
not prematurely, but deliberately, as patterns stabilize and
teams rely on them. Clear naming. Explicit contracts. Decisions
that hold even when context is removed.

Culture carries systems early on. Structure carries them later.

Seen this way, moments of confusion – whether from new team
members or from machines – aren’t failures. They’re indications
that some of the work has been implicit for too long.

The job isn’t to eliminate judgment or flexibility. It’s to
decide which parts need to be explicit so the system can keep
supporting teams as it grows.

Subject: Where Consistency Matters
Body:
For a long time, design system work was framed around the idea of
100% UI consistency. It sounded reassuring. Predictable.
Scalable.

It also set an expectation that was never realistic. Full UI
consistency across a growing organization – across teams,
surfaces, and shifting product contexts – was always out of
reach. Different constraints, different needs, different stages
of maturity. Even with the best intentions, the reality was
messier than the promise.

Consistency is often used as a shortcut for efficiency, and
efficiency is often taken as a sign of quality. But consistency
on its own doesn’t guarantee good outcomes. A perfectly
consistent interface can still be confusing, slow, or wrong for
the job it’s doing.

What actually matters is where consistency shows up.

Design systems earn their value when they’re specific –
consistent interaction patterns, consistent accessibility
guarantees, consistent behavior in common flows. These are the
things users notice and rely on. Visual sameness everywhere else
matters far less, and sometimes actively gets in the way.

This is where teams lose credibility. Promising full consistency
turns every exception into a failure. Instead of learning from
variation, teams end up defending why something doesn’t fit. Over
time, the system hardens, and adoption suffers.

Mature systems make a different trade-off. They aim for high
consistency where it carries real user and team value, and leave
room elsewhere – deliberately. Inconsistency isn’t ignored; it’s
examined.

Seen this way, inconsistency becomes a sign. It can point to gaps
in the system, new product needs, or areas where the work hasn’t
settled yet. Treating it as information, rather than deviation,
keeps the system relevant as the product evolves.

Full UI consistency was never achievable at scale. The real job
is deciding where consistency matters – and having the discipline
to be inconsistent on purpose everywhere else.

The shift doesn’t lower the bar. It raises it.

Subject: Time to Involvement
Body:
There’s a noticeable difference between systems that are healthy
and systems that are tolerated.

In healthy systems, the design system team shows up early. Not to
review work, but to shape it. They’re part of planning
conversations, early sketches, open questions. Their input
changes the direction of the work while it’s still cheap to
change.

In less healthy systems, the system team appears much later.
Often right at the end, when the work is mostly done and the
request is narrow. The role is reduced to validation, not
contribution.

That difference shows up long before adoption numbers change.

Adoption tells you what teams are using. Time to involvement
tells you how teams are approaching the work.

You can see it in small ways. When do teams first reach out? Are
you hearing about work during discovery, or only once it’s built?
Do conversations start with open questions, or finished
solutions?

Over time, those patterns matter more than any single project.

Earlier involvement usually means the system is trusted as a way
to move work forward. Later involvement often means it’s seen as
something to comply with. Both can coexist for a while, which is
why adoption alone can be misleading.

This is also why small changes in how the system team shows up
can make a noticeable difference.

Office hours, shared channels, joining planning sessions – these
don’t immediately change what teams ship. What they change first
is when teams reach out. That shift often shows up before
anything else.

Time to involvement isn’t a KPI to optimize. It’s something worth
paying attention to.

It tells you whether the system is being invited into decisions –
or only asked to approve them.

Subject: The Cost of Early Abstraction
Body:
Design tokens are often described as foundational. Something you
put in place early, before the rest of the system takes shape.

In practice, they tend to arrive much later.

Tokens sit at a level of abstraction that assumes a lot is
already true. Agreement on naming, shared understanding of what
decisions matter, confidence in how changes should ripple through
products. That agreement rarely exists at the start, especially
across design, engineering and product.

Early system work is usually more tentative than that. Teams are
solving immediate problems, patterns are still forming. Language
is inconsistent because the work itself is still unsettled.
Trying to introduce tokens at this stage often surfaces
disagreement faster than it creates alignment.

This is where things start to feel stuck.

Teams debate token structures before they’ve agreed on component
behavior. They argue over semantics without a clear sense of
where variation is actually useful. The work looks sophisticated,
but it’s disconnected from how teams are building day to day.

Systems that gain traction tend to move in the opposite
direction.

They start with something concrete – a component a few teams
genuinely need, a problem that’s already costing time or creating
inconsistency. As those pieces get used, shared decisions begin
to emerge. Certain choices stabilize. Others prove less important
than expected.

That’s the point where abstraction becomes helpful.

Tokens work best when they formalise patterns teams already rely
on. They make existing decisions easier to apply consistently,
not harder to agree on. Introduced too early, they ask
organizations to align on things they haven’t yet experienced.

Seen this way, tokens aren’t a foundation, they’re a signal that
the groundwork has been done.

Start with the work teams are actually doing. Let patterns
settle. Build trust. The abstractions will have something to
stand on when they arrive.

Subject: Setting System Boundaries
Body:
One of the most common sources of design system friction is
unclear boundaries.

Product teams aren’t always sure where the system team’s
responsibility ends and where feature work begins. When that line
is fuzzy, requests linger. Decisions slow down. Conversations
feel heavier than they need to – not because anyone disagrees,
but because ownership is unclear.

Clear boundaries change how teams work together.

A simple example is the “three teams need it” threshold. When a
problem shows up in one or two places, it’s usually best solved
close to the feature – product teams can move quickly without
waiting for system input. When the same issue starts appearing
across multiple teams, it’s a signal that the system may need to
absorb it.

This kind of clarity benefits both sides.

Product teams know when they can act independently. System teams
avoid becoming a bottleneck or a general service desk. Work moves
forward without every decision needing to be escalated.

The tension usually shows up in conversation.

Telling a team that something sits outside the system’s scope can
sound abrupt if it isn’t grounded in shared expectations. What
helps is explaining how scope works, and making it clear that the
system team is paying attention – even when the work stays at the
product level.

Pilots are where these boundaries get exercised.

Early on, system teams often help with work they wouldn’t
normally own. That flexibility helps things get started. Over
time, it becomes important to distinguish what was temporary
support from what’s part of the system’s ongoing responsibility.
Writing that down prevents confusion as more teams get involved.

Healthy boundaries don’t create distance. They make collaboration
easier.

When teams understand where the system ends and product work
begins, they’re more confident making decisions. And the system
team can focus on the work that actually benefits everyone.

Subject: Good Enough to Be Useful
Body:
Design system teams often overestimate how good something needs
to be before it’s useful.

For a product team deciding whether to adopt a component, the bar
for “good enough” is usually lower than system teams expect.
Clarity, availability, and fit often matter more than polish. If
a component solves a real problem and doesn’t create friction,
that’s often enough to get started.

This is where many systems slow themselves down.

Teams spend time refining details, debating edge cases, and
raising internal standards before anything ships. Meanwhile,
product teams keep moving, filling gaps in their own way. The
system becomes carefully crafted, but easy to ignore.

This isn’t because quality doesn’t matter. It’s because quality
works differently over time.

Early on, quality is mostly hypothetical. Decisions are made in
isolation, without real usage to push back. Once a component is
in use, quality becomes concrete. Teams can see where it breaks
down, what causes confusion, and what needs refinement.
Improvements have somewhere to land.

That’s why quality tends to compound after adoption, not before
it.

More mature systems can afford to slow down and raise the bar.
They already have trust, usage, and feedback loops in place. For
teams still trying to earn that trust, prioritizing quality too
early can work against them.

Systems that make it through this stage tend to ship sooner than
feels comfortable, learn from real use, and invest in quality
where it has proven impact.

Adoption doesn’t replace quality. It creates the conditions for
it.

Subject: Scope Before Scale
Body:
Design system teams often try to be generous from the start. The
first component is built to work for every team, every product
area, every possible use case. No one is left out.

That generosity usually comes at a cost.

When a system tries to serve the entire organization from day
one, it has no clear center of gravity. Feedback comes from
everywhere and nowhere. Conflicting needs pile up before any
patterns have settled. Decisions get delayed because every edge
case feels equally valid.

At that stage, teams don’t really know what they’re building yet
– but they’re already accountable to everyone.

Starting smaller changes the kind of learning that’s possible.

Shipping a component for a narrow, contained context forces
focus. You learn who actually needs to be involved in decisions.
You learn how updates get reviewed, approved, and shipped. You
see where product pressure shows up, and where the system bends
or breaks under it.

This is how governance forms in practice, not in theory.

Small starts also shape expectations. When the system is
introduced as intentionally scoped, boundaries feel deliberate.
Expansion becomes something that’s earned through use, not
assumed by default. Saying “not yet” doesn’t feel like exclusion
– it feels like sequencing.

Trying to serve everyone immediately often comes from good
instincts: fairness, ambition, a desire to avoid politics. But
systems don’t scale by being fair to every use case at once. They
scale by being useful somewhere first.

Design systems that last tend to begin with a handful of teams
and a specific problem. They learn what actually matters, fix
what breaks, and grow with evidence instead of confidence.

Scale works better when it’s discovered, not declared.

Subject: Let the Work Lead
Body:
Teams don’t usually stall design system work on purpose. But when
the work is named before it’s experienced, momentum often slows.

Once something is called a “design system”, the conversation
shifts. Leadership starts asking about scope, cost, and return.
Those are reasonable questions. Early on, though, the work
doesn’t have much to point to yet. The outcomes are still
forming. The value hasn’t shown up in a way anyone outside the
team can feel.

At that stage, the burden moves from the work to the explanation.
Progress depends on pitches, not results. Without evidence, the
conversation tends to circle instead of move forward.

The work that gains traction usually follows a different path. A
team ships something useful. A product team moves faster. Fewer
decisions have to be revisited later. Bugs decrease. Timelines
tighten. The impact shows up in delivery before it shows up in
decks.

At that point, naming starts to help. Once people have felt the
benefit, giving the work a name helps connect those outcomes into
something leadership can recognize and support. A design system
becomes investable after it’s already proven useful – not before.

Naming something early can feel like progress. It gives the work
a shape and a story before either really exists. But it also
pulls the conversation away from outcomes and toward
justification.

In practice, adoption tends to follow experience, not
terminology.

Let the work lead. The name will make sense when it’s ready.

Subject: Create an Influence Map
Body:
If your design system is struggling to gain traction, don’t start
by fixing components.

Start by mapping influence.

Because design system adoption is not a meritocracy. It’s
political.

----------------------------
Step 1: Forget the org chart
----------------------------

Org charts show reporting lines. They don’t show decision-making.

Influence lives elsewhere:

* The engineer everyone listens to in reviews
* The designer who sets taste without authority
* The PM who blocks things quietly
* The leader whose opinion ends debates

Write names, not titles.

If you only include managers, your map is already wrong.

-------------------------------------
Step 2: Classify intent, not attitude
-------------------------------------

For each person, ask one question:

If this design system succeeds, do they win or lose?

Then label them:

* Supportive: they benefit if it works
* Undecided: they could go either way
* Opposed: success costs them something

This is not about who’s “nice.” It’s about incentives.

Someone can be pleasant and opposed.

-----------------------------------------
Step 3: Draw influence, not relationships
-----------------------------------------

Now connect people with arrows:

* Thick arrows = strong influence
* Thin arrows = weak influence

Direction matters.

Who shapes whose opinion? Who defers to whom? Who can override
whom?

This is where the real system reveals itself.

--------------------------------
Step 4: Find the leverage points
--------------------------------

Look for:

* Supportive people with strong influence
* Undecided people influenced by supporters
* Opponents who don’t actually influence decisions

That’s your strategy.

You don’t need everyone on board. You need the right alliances.

Trying to convince the wrong people is how design systems stall.

-------------------------
The point most teams miss
-------------------------

Influence maps aren’t political games. They’re clarity tools.

They stop you from:

* Over-investing in buy-in that doesn’t matter
* Fighting resistance you can’t win
* Assuming logic alone will carry the day

Design systems don’t spread because they’re correct. They spread
because the system around them allows it.

Map that system first.

Subject: Design System Siblings
Body:
Here‘s one of the simplest ways to explain design systems to
confused stakeholders:

Put them in a family.

Specifically, the family of enterprise-wide tools that shape how
companies build, ship, publish, and maintain digital experiences.

Design systems have siblings. And understanding those siblings
makes the role of a design system much easier to grasp.

If your partners—engineering, product, marketing, content,
leadership—aren’t clear on what a design system is, they’ll fill
in the gaps with assumptions:

* “Isn’t this just a CMS?”
* “Our CRM already handles that.”
* “We have a DAM; why do we need this?”
* “Is this like a UI kit from Figma?”
* “Wait… who owns this?”

When a design system is misunderstood, it gets mis-scoped,
underfunded, and misused.

When it’s understood, it becomes a strategic asset.

Most teams jump straight into defining components, tokens,
accessibility, governance, or contribution.

But not the first thing your stakeholders need.

They need context.

Without context, a design system feels like “another platform,”
“another tool,” or “another thing to maintain.” So teams end up
explaining too tactically, too technically, or too narrowly.

Instead, the fastest way to clarity is contrast. Explain what a
design system is by showing what it is not, next to the tools it
sits beside.

A design system finally makes sense when people see it as a
sibling—not a duplicate—of the enterprise platforms they already
know.

Here’s how to teach the siblings:

CMS (Content Management System)

* What it manages: Pages, posts, structured content
* Who uses it: Marketing, content ops, publishers
* Its job: Publish and organize information
* How it relates: The CMS distributes content; the design system
standardizes the UI used to display it.

CRM (Customer Relationship Management)

* What it manages: Customer data, interactions, segmentation
* Who uses it: Sales, CX, lifecycle marketing
* Its job: Show you who the user is
* How it relates: The CRM knows who the user is; the design
system shapes how experiences feel to that user.

DAM (Digital Asset Management)

* What it manages: Images, video, brand assets, documents
* Who uses it: Creative teams, marketing, brand
* Its job: Store and distribute brand assets
* How it relates: The DAM holds assets; the design system
provides instructions and components for using them consistently.

PIM (Product Information Management)

* What it manages: Product data, specs, variations
* Who uses it: E-commerce, product operations
* Its job: Maintain product metadata
* How it relates: The PIM manages product content; the design
system manages product presentation.

Design System

* What it manages: UI components, patterns, tokens, guidelines
* Who uses it: Design, engineering, content designers,
accessibility teams
* Its job: Create a shared visual and functional language across
the entire digital ecosystem
* Its unique value: ensures surfaces are consistent, accessible,
and cohesive.

---------------------------------
Bonus Framing: The Family Summary
---------------------------------

* CMS: What we say
* CRM: Who we say it to
* DAM: What we use to say it
* PIM: What we sell
* Design System: How it looks, behaves, and works

When stakeholders see this picture, design systems stop feeling
abstract and start feeling essential.

Subject: Design Systems as Decision Engines
Body:
Most teams still treat design systems like collections of
components. That’s the shallow end of the pool. The real
value—the part that actually scales—isn’t in buttons or tokens.
It’s in decisions.

A design system that only gives teams assets forces them to
guess. A design system that gives teams decision clarity makes
them faster, more consistent, and dramatically more aligned.

That’s why the systems that win aren’t libraries. They’re
decision engines.

-------------------
The Real Bottleneck
-------------------

Every slow workflow inside a product team can be traced back to
one thing: people making decisions with too little context or too
many options.

* Which spacing is correct?
* What pattern fits this flow?
* Who approves?
* Why is this team doing it differently?
* Why didn’t engineering interpret the design the same way?

Components don’t solve these problems.
​Shared decision logic does.

-------------------------------------
The Three Layers of a Decision Engine
-------------------------------------

* Standards (the rules). Clear, opinionated standards remove
subjective debates. They give everyone the same baseline for what
“good” means.
* Guidance (the reasoning). This is the multiplier. Guidance
explains why a decision is correct, creating trust and alignment.
Done well, it replaces hours of Slack back-and-forth.
* Assets (the tools). Components, tokens, templates.
Essential—but only valuable when built on standards and guidance.
Otherwise they’re just parts in a drawer.

---------------------------------
The Shift That Changes Everything
---------------------------------

Stop centralizing control.
Start centralizing clarity.

When teams understand how to make the right decisions without
asking permission, everything scales:

* Fewer redesigns
* Fewer inconsistencies
* Fewer meetings
* Faster engineering velocity
* Better cross-team alignment

Your design system’s job is not to be a gatekeeper.
Its job is to make product teams self-sufficient.

--------------------------------
If You Want a System That Scales
--------------------------------

Treat every asset you create as a vehicle for one question:

“What decision does this help someone make faster?”

If you answer that consistently, your design system won’t just
unify your UI.
It will transform how your organization ships.

That’s the real value.
That’s the part that scales.

Subject: Design Systems Emerge
Body:
Reading time: around 1 minute

Design systems are rarely the result of a single brilliant plan.

They’re the outcome of a hundred small decisions, tested and
adjusted over time.

That’s what business consultant Henry Mintzberg called emergent
strategy: patterns that form through action, not intention. In
1987, he wrote in Harvard Business Review (

):

“Deliberate strategy precludes learning once the strategy is
formulated; emergent strategy fosters it.”
That’s design systems in a nutshell.

Most teams try to “build the system” in a vacuum: collecting
buttons, colors, and spacing tokens like ingredients before
baking a cake.

But that’s not how systems really come together. The truth is,
the list of components isn’t a recipe. It’s a reflection of
what’s already been learned in the organization.

Creating a design system is more like restoring an antique car.
You take it apart to see what still works, what needs tuning, and
what’s beyond repair. At some point, you’re left with every part
scattered across your garage floor… old and new, dusty and
shining.

That’s your system: a collection of pieces waiting to be
reassembled into something that actually runs.

A successful design system doesn’t begin with a blueprint. It
begins with a teardown or what already exists.

Because only after you’ve deconstructed what is can you design
what could be.

Subject: Design System Inertia
Body:
One of the biggest challenges in design system work isn’t what to
build.

It’s how to get it moving.

If you’ve ever tried to start a design system from scratch, you
know what inertia feels like.

Meetings stall.

Funding fades.

Everyone agrees it’s a “good idea,” but no one’s quite sure when
to start.

The truth is, this isn’t a design problem. It’s a physics
problem.

Isaac Newton’s first law of motion says that objects at rest tend
to stay at rest.

In organizational terms: teams without a design system will
likely keep working without one.

But here’s the good news: that same law gives you the solution.
Objects in motion tend to stay in motion.

If you want your design system to move, link it to something
that’s already moving.

What’s always moving in a digital organization?

Product work.

Roadmaps are alive.

Teams have budgets, deadlines, and executive buy-in.

That’s momentum… and your design system can hitch a ride.

In most companies, there are natural inflection points that
already signal big change:

* Rebrands
* Replatforms
* Re-orgs
* Content migrations
* Mergers

Moments where the company says, “While we’re doing this, we might
as well do it right.”

That’s your opening.

Volunteer your team.

Step into the change.

Use that energy to quietly start laying the groundwork for your
system.

And here’s the twist: you don’t even need to say the words
“design system.”

To leadership, that phrase can sound abstract. Or worse: like
extra work.

So talk about outcomes instead. Say things like:

* “Doing work this way puts us six weeks ahead of schedule.”
* “See how many fewer bugs we had by reusing code that’s already
been QA’d?”
* “Our velocity has increased over the last few sprints.”

They’ll feel the impact before they even know it’s a design
system.

Because at the end of the day, momentum isn’t built by convincing
people.

It’s built by delivering wins inside the work that’s already in
motion.

So, don’t fight inertia.

Use it.

Find the moving train, and get on board.

Subject: Wireframe to Design in One Click
Body:
Remember Axure? Balsamiq? OmniGraffle? The wireframing tools that
made rough low fidelity digital mockups feel fast and
approachable? These tools were great for getting collaborative
input from people that weren’t necessarily designers, but the
tradeoff was always the same… you’d wireframe screens in
Balsamiq, get alignment, then rebuild everything from scratch in
Figma.

What if you could get that same speed but using your actual
design system components? What if you could switch between low
fidelity and high fidelity with one click?

That’s exactly what we built for the New York State Design System
(

). Using a clever approach to Figma variable collections and
modes, designers can switch components from low fidelity mode to
high fidelity mode. And it works in both directions. Start low
fidelity wireframing in FigJam and copy/paste into Figma for a
high-fidelity view. Or start in high fidelity in Figma, and
switch down to low fidelity to keep stakeholder feedback focused
on layout and content.

-----------------
What’s The Value?
-----------------

Before diving into how it works, let’s talk about a few reasons
why we implemented this.

First, we had several clients who didn’t want to take the time to
wireframe the full user flows. They saw the work as duplicative,
and instructed designers to start work in high fidelity to save
time. This new approach helped us reduce this roadblock to
wireframing.

The second reason had to do with getting the right kind of input
and feedback at the right time. If a designer reviews a
high-fidelity screen with clients, the feedback is usually some
variation of “hm… can we try that button in blue?” That might be
the feedback they’re looking for, but probably not, especially
early on. Switch the same design to low fidelity before the
review, and the feedback might be more like, “hm… should this
content come before or after the image?” Better feedback.

There’s another, more pragmatic, benefit for a large org: FigJam
licenses are cheaper, and FigJam files can even be opened to
stakeholders for 24-hour sessions without requiring accounts. You
can run a consensus-gathering workshop with 30 people across
departments by giving them temporary FigJam access, sharing the
low fidelity components as sticker sheets (for example), and
letting them contribute ideas directly. Because stakeholders are
working with actual components, they can be instantly upgraded to
high fidelity once decisions are made. This can add up to
significant cost savings (both money and time) for large teams.

​

--------------------------------------
How Does It Work? (The Simple Version)
--------------------------------------

My explanation is going to assume you have a solid understanding
of how Figma Variables work. If you need a primer, Figma has a
solid introduction for beginners (

).

The way we’ve implemented Fidelity Modes is based on using
Figma’s variable collections as a sort of routing system. Think
of it like a switchboard that can send different values to the
same components depending on which mode you’re in.

The simplest setup is based on three types of variables:

* Primitive Variables: Raw values, like specific color hex codes,
pixel sizes, etc.
* Routing Variables: The “switchboard” that says, “in low
fidelity mode, use this primitive; in high fidelity mode, use
that one.”
* Applied Variables: These are the variables that actually get
assigned to your component properties.

For fidelity switching, we created a routing collection with
three modes: Low, Mid, and High. When you switch modes, all of
your components automatically update because they’re pulling from
variables that now point to different values.

For example, when the mode is set to Low Fidelity, the border
radius variables are all set to 0. The color variables are all
translated to a grayscale equivalent. The font family variables
are set to Comic Sans. In High Fidelity, components are rendered
in full detail including accurate brand colors and fonts. Same
components, different modes.

We also built in a second Fidelity routing mode for designers to
change the Fidelity font from hand drawn (Comic Sans) to
scribbled lines (Redacted Script). This is for times when a
designer wants to focus solely on layout and take the focus
entirely away from text content. For the Fidelity modes to work
with the text in your components and screens, you need to build
and publish Figma Text Styles from your Applied Variables
collection.

​
A key thing to understand is that you don’t need to expose this
complexity to the designers using your design system. You only
need to publish the Applied variable collection. Teams just use
the components normally… they don’t need to understand the
routing logic that happens behind the scenes. They can even build
new components from the published Applied variable collection and
their new components will automatically get the Fidelity
switching functionality. The design system team handles the
complexity once, and everyone else just gets a ✨magical✨ new
feature.

-----------------
FigJam Workaround
-----------------

The first time we tried this, we ran into the issue where FigJam
doesn’t let you set modes.

But we figured out a workaround. Create a section (not a frame)
in Figma, set it to Low or Mid Fidelity, and copy it into FigJam.
Any components you place in that section will inherit the
fidelity mode setting. We tried using Frames as artboards in
FigJam and found that there were issues with variable modes not
being correctly applied when components were placed on top of
Frames inside of Sections. Use sections as your FigJam artboards,
and you’re good to go.

--------------------
Should You Try This?
--------------------

This approach requires a well-structured foundation. You need a
thoughtful set of design tokens (you can find ours here (

)) implemented in Figma as variables, a library of components
built from those variables, and a systematic approach to
extending. It's not for everyone, especially if you've already
built many components.

If your design system is in its infancy or you’d like to take
your system to the next level, start by building your components
from a single-mode variable collection. If you have multiple
brands put those in a lower level collection and map your Applied
Variables to the brand collection. By taking this approach it
becomes easy to insert additional routing layers, like Fidelity
Modes, in the future.

Fidelity modes feel like a superpower. To designers who see it
for the first time, it feels like a magic trick… but it’s really
just a clever use of variables.

--------------
One More Thing
--------------

We’re also using this routing collection approach for more than
just fidelity. The same routing technique can be used for:

* Multiple brands (useful if different teams need customized
versions of components)
* Device sizes (switch between mobile, tablet, and desktop
styling)
* Appearance modes (light, dark, high contrast)

For example, we’re planning to add an “Organization” routing
collection so that once the NYS Design System is published to the
community, local governments in New York can plug in their own
brand colors and customize it without starting from scratch.

Credit for the routing collection concept to Vincent Schlothauer
(

) and Franziska Haaf (

) from the REWE design system team, whose talk at Into Design
Systems 2025 inspired the idea for this.

-------------------
Want to Learn More?
-------------------

This workflow is still in beta, but we’re excited about where
it's heading. If you’re experimenting with similar ideas or have
questions, you can reach out to us at designsystem@its.ny.gov (

).

You can try out this technique in the Figma community file (

) we published.

The NYS Design System was recently named a finalist for the 2025
Zeroheight Award for Innovation for this fidelity-switching
approach. If this article or technique resonated with you, we'd
be grateful for your vote: The NYS Design System: Using Routing
Variables to Allow DS-driven FigJam Prototyping (

)

—Leo Vogel (

), UX Lead, NYS Design System Team

Whenever you’re ready, there are 3 ways we can help you level up
your design system skills:

* Beginner: start with our free course “Make Design Systems
People Want to Use (

)” for an overview of what it takes to succeed in design systems.
* Intermediate: Go through our “Design Systems 101 (

)” course, a hands-on, 72-module, 10+ hour course that gets you

* Advanced: register for an upcoming live cohort of “Design

Subject: Why Product Teams Don’t Contribute to Design Systems
Body:
Design systems thrive on contributions from across an
organization.

Without contributions, design systems become bottlenecks: stale,
disconnected, and increasingly irrelevant to product realities.

The irony? The same teams you built the system for are often the
least likely to contribute back.

---------------------------------
The Hard Truth Most DS Teams Miss
---------------------------------

Most design system teams assume product teams should want to
contribute.

But that assumption ignores two key truths.

First, too many new things. A 2006 Duke study showed that over
40% of human actions are habitual. So when you introduce new
tools, processes, or workflows, you’re not just changing
software. You’re asking people to rewire their habits.

Second, it’s not their job. Product teams exist to ship products.
Not to maintain or improve your design system. When deadlines
loom, “contribute to the design system” naturally slides to the
bottom of the backlog.

---------------------------------------------------
Flip the Frame: Don’t Ask for Help, Remove Friction
---------------------------------------------------

If you want product teams to contribute, stop asking them to act
like design system teams.
Your goal isn’t to convince them; it’s to make contribution the
path of least resistance.

Here’s what actually works:

* Reduce cognitive load. Integrate your system into their natural
workflow. Don’t force adoption through extra steps or tools.
* Reward contributions. Recognize and celebrate even small pull
requests or component updates.
* Design for them, not you. Treat product teams as your primary
customers and make contribution feel like progress, not charity.
* Automate the boring parts. The easier it is to contribute, the
more contributions you’ll see.
* Align incentives. If contributing helps them hit deadlines
faster, you won’t need to beg for buy-in.

------------------------------------
The Real Job of a Design System Team
------------------------------------

Your job isn’t to collect components. It’s to create alignment.

Every contribution from a product team is a signal that they
trust your system, that it helps them move faster, that it feels
theirs.

When contribution becomes collaboration, your design system stops
being a product and starts becoming a culture.

That’s when it scales.

—Dan Mall, founder of Design System University

Whenever you’re ready, there are 3 ways we can help you level up
your design system skills:

People Want to Use (

)” for an overview of what it takes to succeed in design systems.
* Intermediate: Go through our “Design Systems 101 (

)” course, a hands-on, 72-module, 10+ hour course that gets you
started on everything you need to know to get a design system’s
design and code up and running.
* Advanced: register for an upcoming live cohort of “Design
System in 90 Days (

)” to learn how to set up a design system practice at your
